one_line_takeaway: Iteratively re-optimizing adversarial patches against repeatedly adversarially-trained YOLOv10 detectors produces patches that are more damaging and more transferable than single-round (1st-order) patches, and shows that adversarial training which forgets earlier-order patches (Non-Successive) leaves exploitable diversity gaps that only retaining the full patch history (Successive) closes -- yet even Successive hardening never fully restores clean/grayscale-level accuracy.
key_finding: Baseline (Table 1): across all four settings/orders 0-5, clean AP_Person@[.5:.95] stays essentially flat around 0.58-0.59, and AP under 11 grayscale patch levels stays around 0.52-0.54, confirming order-to-order model changes do not by themselves change unattacked detector accuracy. In the main cat-and-mouse heatmaps (Delta_AP = AP_Grayscale - AP): Non-Successive (k=1) shows uniformly poor robustness regardless of patch/model order (max Delta_AP=0.16, mean 0.10+/-0.06), and higher-order patches are on average more damaging than 1st-order patches even against the same-order model. Non-Successive (k=3) improves robustness on the model=patch_order+1 diagonal but for patch orders 1-4, the model of order (patch_order+1) performs worse than the model of order (patch_order) -- evidence of 'insufficient patch diversity' when only the latest order's patches are retained during training. Successive training (k=1 and k=3) removes this diversity deficit; Successive (k=3) yields higher overall AP than Successive (k=1) even for lower-order networks. Transferability study across 21 unhardened COCO-pretrained YOLOv9/10/11/12 detectors: patches from Successive-trained orders show a 'wavy downward' AP trend with increasing order (i.e., growing transfer strength), and for patch orders 3, 4, and 6, Successive(k=3) patches achieve lower AP (more damage) than Successive(k=1) patches, supporting that k=3 training does not simply produce weaker patches. In Non-Successive cases, transferred patch impact trends upward with order, and 2nd-order patches are markedly more damaging than 1st-order in transfer, in contrast to their same-order robustness gains.
core_contribution: The paper studies what happens when adversarial-patch attacks and adversarial training against real-time object detectors are played as an iterated 'cat-and-mouse game': an (n+1)-th-order patch is optimized to defeat an n-th-order adversarially-trained detector, and the resulting patch is then used to train an (n+1)-th-order detector, and so on. Using YOLOv10b as the reference detector and person-class evasion patches, the authors run this loop up to order 5-6 under four training regimes that vary (a) how many new patches are optimized per order (k=1 vs k=3) and (b) whether all previous-order patches are kept in adversarial training ('Successive') or only the latest-order patch is used ('Non-Successive'). They report that higher-order patches generalize/transfer better and are more damaging on average than 1st-order patches, that higher-order (repeatedly hardened) models achieve higher AP than lower-order models on average, but that adversarial training never fully restores performance to the clean/grayscale baseline, and that Non-Successive training (dropping earlier-order patches) leaves 'insufficient patch diversity' -- for patches of order 1-4, models of order (patch_order+1) actually perform worse than models of order patch_order, an effect that Successive training (retaining all prior-order patches) removes.
threat_model: White-box evasion attack against a real-time object detector (YOLOv10b): the attacker has full access to detector weights and optimizes a 256x256 printable/overlay adversarial patch (via an augmentation pipeline of color jitter, rotation in [-30,30] degrees, perspective transform, and random resizing) that is placed inside the bounding box of a 'Person' instance to suppress that detection, using an objectness/confidence loss plus smoothness and color-validity regularizers (L = lambda_obj*L_obj + lambda_smt*L_smt + lambda_val*L_val). A secondary gray-box transferability scenario is also evaluated, where patches optimized against one detector/order are tested against 21 other COCO-pretrained detectors (YOLOv9/10/11/12 family) with no access to those target models beyond the shared training dataset. The defender's countermeasure is patch-aware adversarial training: with probability pi=0.25 a patch is inserted at a random position inside a ground-truth person bounding box during YOLOv10 training.
limitations: The authors explicitly flag: (1) they do not control for distribution shift introduced by differing datasets/parameterizations -- detectors are trained/evaluated on COCO while patches are optimized on INRIAPerson, and resize ranges differ between patch optimization (0.3-0.6) and adversarial training (0.75-0.9); (2) different patch hyperparameters (resize range, learning rate, epochs, optimizer) could yield stronger patches, so reported effect sizes are not hyperparameter-optimal; (3) experiments cover only the Person class even though detectors are trained on the full 80-class COCO taxonomy; (4) each single patch optimization takes ~3.5 hours on an RTX 4090 + Intel i9-7980XE, which is a stated technical bottleneck that prevented exploring more than ~5-6 orders and forced any further scaling toward reduced input dimensionality; (5) the authors state a clear trend/prediction for arbitrarily higher orders 'is hardly possible' from their data, only a 'cautious' one; (6) the paper does not investigate whether the cat-and-mouse process converges or diverges, leaving that to future work.

datasets_used: COCO (train2017 for detector/adversarial training, val2017 for evaluation)
INRIA Person (train split, positive images, for patch optimization)
quantitative_results: Baseline clean AP_Person@[.5:.95] (APclean) roughly constant at 0.58-0.59 across orders 0-5 in all four settings (Table 1)
Baseline AP under 11 grayscale patch levels (APGrayscale) roughly constant at 0.52-0.54 across orders 0-5 (Table 1); std dev below 0.01
Non-Successive (k=1): max Delta_AP = 0.16, mean Delta_AP = 0.10 +/- 0.06 across the patch-order x model-order heatmap
Non-Successive (k=3): for patch orders 1-4, model order = patch_order+1 underperforms model order = patch_order (diagonal inversion attributed to insufficient patch diversity)
Successive (k=1) and Successive (k=3): diagonal inversion effect removed; Successive (k=3) shows higher overall AP than Successive (k=1) even at lower model orders

Transferability study: 21 COCO-pretrained YOLOv9/10/11/12 detectors (all order-0/unhardened) evaluated per patch order; Successive(k=3) patches of order 3, 4, and 6 achieve lower AP (i.e., are more damaging) than Successive(k=1) patches of the same order
Patch optimization cost: ~3.5 hours per patch on an Nvidia RTX 4090 + Intel i9-7980XE
baselines_compared: Grayscale-patch baseline (11 grayscale levels applied instead of adversarial patches) used as the reference for Delta_AP
Four adversarial-training regimes compared against each other: Non-Successive k=1, Successive k=1, Non-Successive k=3, Successive k=3
21 externally pretrained, unhardened COCO detectors from the YOLOv9/10/11/12 families used as transfer targets
